common_name: Yellow Ipe (Brazilian Trumpet Tree, Yellow Lapacho, Guayacan Amarillo)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in full sun with well-drained soil; Handroanthus serratifolius is moderately cold-hardy, surviving temperatures to 26–28°F in established specimens, making it viable in protected zone 9b-10 locations. The tree blooms most spectacularly on bare branches in spring after a dry period — withhold irrigation through February to trigger maximum bloom. The serrated leaflets (the 'serratifolius' — serrated leaves) distinguish this species from related Handroanthus species. Drought-tolerant once established. One of the national symbols of Brazil.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil — Yellow Ipe is drought-tolerant once established and performs well in the sandy, well-drained soils common throughout Gulf Coast and Florida landscapes. Handroanthus serratifolius is a large, deciduous to semi-deciduous tree native to Venezuela, Colombia, Peru, Bolivia, and Brazil in the Bignoniaceae family, one of several South American species collectively called 'Ipê Amarelo' (Yellow Ipe) — celebrated as among the most spectacular flowering trees in the world. In late winter to spring, the tree drops its leaves and then, on the leafless branches, produces one of the most dramatic flower displays in all of the tree kingdom: the entire canopy simultaneously explodes in masses of large, 3-inch, brilliant golden-yellow, trumpet-shaped flowers covering every branch without a single leaf to distract from the flower display — the tree appears from a distance as a cloud of pure, intense yellow gold. After the bloom, the new leaves emerge as the flowers fall, carpeting the ground with a golden layer of spent petals. The wood of Ipê (called 'ironwood' in lumber trade) is one of the hardest, most durable timbers in the world — rated Class A for marine and ground-contact use and used for heavy decking, bridges, and structural applications across South America and in exported form worldwide. In the ornamental world, the bloom display alone justifies the tree's cultivation in zone 9-12 landscapes.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Handroanthus serratifolius (Yellow Ipe) for Florida
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Handroanthus species for Gulf Coast zone 9-10
LSU AgCenter — Tropical Flowering Trees for Gulf Coast Louisiana
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Handroanthus serratifolius

common_name: Yellow Trumpet Vine (Chamberlain's Anemopaegma, Golden Bignonia)

planting.note: Plant container-grown vines in spring on a strong trellis, arbor, or fence in full sun to partial shade; Anemopaegma chamberlaynii grows vigorously in zone 9-10 and may freeze back to the ground in zone 9a winters but regrows from the root system. The vine climbs by tendrils and can reach 20–30 feet on adequate support. Flowers appear primarily in spring and fall, resembling yellow Allamanda or Trumpet vine flowers. Prune after each bloom cycle to maintain shape. Propagate from cuttings or seed.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile soil — Yellow Trumpet Vine blooms most prolifically in full sun and produces fewer flowers and more vegetative growth in shaded positions. Anemopaegma chamberlaynii is a vigorous, tendril-climbing, woody vine native to Brazil in the Bignoniaceae family, grown for its display of large, bright yellow, trumpet-shaped flowers that closely resemble those of Allamanda or the yellow form of Bignonia capreolata. The flowers are large (2–3 inches long), funnel-shaped to trumpet-shaped, bright golden-yellow, with five spreading, slightly frilled petal lobes — produced in terminal and axillary clusters throughout the spring and fall. The vine has attractive, pinnately compound leaves with 2 leaflets and a terminal tendril that grips supports for climbing. In zone 9-10 Gulf Coast conditions, Anemopaegma chamberlaynii is valued as a less common but ornamentally rewarding alternative to more frequently planted Bignoniaceae vines like Bignonia capreolata and Campsis radicans, producing the same general 'trumpet vine' aesthetic but in a pure, clear yellow. The genus name 'Anemopaegma' comes from the Greek for 'playing in the wind' — referring to the delicate, light movement of the flowers in a breeze. A handsome and underused flowering vine for zone 9-11 fences and arbors.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Bignoniaceae vines for Florida landscapes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tropical vines for Gulf Coast zone 9-10
LSU AgCenter — Ornamental Vines for Gulf Coast Louisiana Landscapes
Missouri Botanical Garden — Anemopaegma chamberlaynii species profile

common_name: African Trumpet Tree (Magnificent Fernandoa, Yellow African Trumpet)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in full sun with well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Fernandoa magnifica is frost-sensitive and suited to zone 10-11 outdoor planting. The tree is relatively fast-growing and can begin blooming within 3–5 years from seed. The enormous clusters of large yellow-orange trumpet flowers make it one of the most impressive African ornamental trees for tropical landscapes. Semi-deciduous — it briefly drops leaves before the main spring bloom. Drought-tolerant once established on well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil — African Trumpet Tree is drought-tolerant once established and grows vigorously in the well-drained, sandy soils of tropical coastal landscapes. Fernandoa magnifica is a medium-sized, semi-deciduous flowering tree from eastern tropical Africa (Kenya, Tanzania, Mozambique, Zimbabwe) in the Bignoniaceae family, belonging to the same remarkable family that gave horticulture Jacaranda, Tabebuia, Catalpa, and Bignonia. The tree produces large, pinnately compound leaves and bears its flowers in massive, terminal, many-flowered panicles at the tips of the branches: each individual flower is a large (2–3 inch), funnel to broadly trumpet-shaped bloom in vivid yellow-orange to orange, with five spreading, frilled petal lobes and a slightly darker orange interior. When a Fernandoa magnifica flowers — typically in spring, often producing the display on leafless or partially leafless branches — the sheer density and size of the flower clusters is extraordinary, covering the branch tips in orange-yellow bloom. The species is a significant ornamental tree in tropical East African gardens and in tropical botanical collections worldwide, considered one of the finest flowering trees of the African continent in terms of bloom impact. Underused in zone 10-11 US tropical landscapes compared to its merit.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Fernandoa magnifica species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — African ornamental trees for South Florida
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Fernandoa magnifica cultivation notes
South African National Biodiversity Institute — Fernandoa magnifica

common_name: Foxtail Palm (Wodyetia Palm, Australian Foxtail Palm)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in full sun with very well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Foxtail Palm is cold-sensitive and suited to zone 10-11 outdoor planting in the US, though it may survive brief temperature dips to 28–30°F as a mature tree. It grows moderately fast in warm, humid conditions. The distinctive arching, full, brush-like fronds (the 'foxtail') give the palm an exceptionally lush appearance. Self-cleaning (old fronds drop cleanly). Provide magnesium and potassium supplements to prevent nutritional deficiencies in Florida and Gulf Coast soils.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil — Foxtail Palm grows most vigorously in full sun and becomes thin and elongated in shaded positions. Wodyetia bifurcata is a medium-sized, single-trunked feather palm endemic to a small area of far northeastern Queensland, Australia (Cape York Peninsula) in the Arecaceae family, first described to science in 1978 and named for the Aboriginal elder Wodyeti, the last person to possess traditional knowledge of the species. The palm has become one of the most widely planted ornamental palms in tropical and subtropical gardens worldwide since its introduction, for one primary reason: the extraordinary, immediately distinctive form of its fronds. Each frond arches gracefully outward and is densely clothed with leaflets that radiate in multiple planes around the rachis in a full, brush-like, three-dimensional arrangement that creates the appearance of an enormous, lush foxtail — a completely different silhouette from the flat, two-dimensional frond arrangement typical of most pinnate palms. The combination of the lush foxtail fronds, the clean, smooth, gray-ringed trunk, and the self-cleaning habit (old fronds drop without persistent bases) makes the Foxtail Palm one of the most elegant and low-maintenance ornamental palms available for zone 10-12 landscapes. Moderate growth rate; reaches 20–30 feet at maturity.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Wodyetia bifurcata (Foxtail Palm) for Florida landscapes
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Wodyetia bifurcata cultivation guide
Queensland Government — Wodyetia bifurcata native range and conservation
International Palm Society — Wodyetia bifurcata: cultivation in zone 10-11

common_name: Mazari Palm (Dwarf Fan Palm, Ritchie's Nannorrhops, Afghan Palm)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in full sun with extremely well-drained, sandy to gravelly soil; Nannorrhops ritchiana is one of the most cold-hardy fan palms, rated to zone 7 and reportedly surviving temperatures to 10–15°F in its native habitat. It requires excellent drainage — root rot in moist, poorly-drained soils is the primary cause of failure. Grows naturally in hot, arid, rocky desert conditions and is extremely drought-tolerant. The clumping habit produces multiple trunks from a shared root mass. Growth is slow. This is a genuinely rare palm in US cultivation despite its exceptional cold hardiness.

directions: Plant in full sun in extremely well-drained, sandy, gravelly, or rocky soil — Mazari Palm is adapted to the arid, rocky mountain deserts of Central Asia and demands the best drainage possible, making raised beds or slope planting essential in humid Gulf Coast conditions. Nannorrhops ritchiana is a clumping, multi-stemmed fan palm native to Afghanistan, Pakistan, Iran, and adjacent regions of Central Asia in the Arecaceae family — one of the most cold-hardy and drought-tolerant of all fan palms and a genuinely remarkable species for gardeners in cold climates who want to grow a palm outdoors. The plant grows naturally at elevations up to 6,000 feet in the arid mountain ranges of Afghanistan and Pakistan, surviving both intense summer heat (110°F+) and winter cold (10–15°F), conditions more extreme than most other palm species can endure. The plant produces a clumping mass of stems that branch from a common base (one of the few monocots that branch), each stem terminating in a rounded crown of stiff, silvery blue-green to gray-green fan leaves 2–3 feet wide with sharply divided segments. The overall appearance is compact, dense, and distinctly silver-gray — quite unlike the tropical-green appearance of most fan palms. Nannorrhops ritchiana blooms terminally — after a trunk blooms (once per stem, after many years), that stem dies but the clump continues via other stems. The fiber of the leaves and stems is used in traditional Central Asian crafts. Exceptional cold hardiness makes it valuable for zone 7-9 palm enthusiasts.
sources: International Palm Society — Nannorrhops ritchiana cold hardiness notes
University of Arizona Cooperative Extension — Cold-Hardy Palms for the Southwest
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Nannorrhops ritchiana cultivation
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Nannorrhops ritchiana species profile
